summary: The first_proof_2 dataset comprises 10 research-level mathematics problems, of which 6 (prob-01 through prob-05 and prob-07) each have a resolved proof issue and zero open issues, indicating strong recent traction. The remaining problems (prob-06, prob-08, prob-09, prob-10) show no recorded issue activity, suggesting they are untouched or unstarted. The proof-status counts (100 issues per q1-q10) appear to reflect a uniform tracking metric rather than differentiated progress.
problems: No issue activity recorded for prob-06, prob-08, prob-09, and prob-10 — these problems appear unstarted or untracked.
The uniform 'q1-q10: 100 issues' proof-status counts lack differentiation, making it hard to gauge true per-problem progress from this metric alone.
Resolved problems (prob-01 to prob-05, prob-07) have no open follow-up or verification issues, raising the question of whether resolutions have been independently reviewed.
highlights: Six of ten problems (prob-01, prob-02, prob-03, prob-04, prob-05, prob-07) have a resolved proof and zero open issues — a 60% completion rate.
Resolved problems span diverse domains: descriptive set theory, piecewise-linear geometry, probability, Riemannian geometry (k-dilation), unique ergodicity, and quasi-reduced words.
Clean issue ledgers (0 open across resolved problems) indicate efficient closure with no lingering blockers.

suggested_todos.title: Independently verify the proofs for the six resolved problems (prob-01 to prob-05, prob-07) and log review issues
Kick off work on the four untouched problems (prob-06, prob-08, prob-09, prob-10) by drafting initial scoping issues
Clarify the meaning of the uniform 'q1-q10: 100 issues' proof-status metric and replace with differentiated progress tracking
Prioritize prob-06 next, as it is the only gap among the otherwise-completed prob-01 through prob-07 sequence
Document resolution methods from completed problems to accelerate the remaining four
